metadata.summary: Formal 7-step process to end a project with signed acceptance, closed contracts, lessons learned, archived docs, final report, operations handover, and team recognition.

content.title: Project Closure
content.summary: A formal 7-step process to end a project: verify deliverable acceptance with signed artefacts, close financial and contract obligations, conduct lessons learned before the team disperses, archive all documentation, produce a final report, hand over to operations, and recognise the team. Closure applies equally to normal completion, cancelled projects, and phase-end gates in multi-phase programmes. Every checklist item is a record with status, owner, and evidence — not a checkbox that an agent can self-certify.

why.p: Projects without formal closure leave resources allocated to "finished" work, contracts accruing costs, access permissions open, and institutional knowledge lost when teams scatter. The most-skipped phase is the most expensive omission: a missed handover creates operational incidents; a missed lessons-learned session means the next project repeats the same errors.

list.item: Project objectives met and final deliverables accepted (normal closure).
Project cancelled mid-flight — still close formally to recover value, release resources, capture lessons.
Phase-end of a multi-phase programme before the next phase receives funding.
Agency engagement final invoice and warranty handover.
Absorption of a legacy project into BAU operations.
Project still has open scope, unresolved issues, or unsigned acceptance — resolve those first.
Operational or run-rate work with no defined endpoint — use service transition documents instead.
Long-tail support work after handover — that is operations, not project closure.

section.name: step-1-verify-deliverable-acceptance
section.p: Obtain written sign-off from the accepting authority for every deliverable before declaring the project closed. Silence does not equal acceptance.

rule.statement: Obtain written sign-off from the accepting authority for every deliverable before declaring the project closed. Silence does not equal acceptance.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous completion status creates re-opened projects, warranty disputes, and resource limbo. A signed artefact is the only auditable evidence of acceptance.

rule.statement: Acceptance criteria must be observable and agreed before closure starts — they cannot be written retrospectively to match the delivered output.
rule.rationale: Retrospective criteria are subjective and always pass; they destroy the value of the gate.

section.name: step-2-administrative-closure
section.p: Complete financial, resource, and contract closure activities to release allocated capital and permissions.

list.item: Financial: process final invoices, close purchase orders, return unused budget, reconcile final costs (flag delayed net-60 vendor invoices as pending — do not auto-zero).
Resource: release team members, return equipment, revoke access permissions, notify resource managers.
Contract: send termination letters, archive agreements, complete warranty/support handover.

rule.statement: Sequence access revocation after operations handover acknowledgement — never revoke before the receiving team confirms readiness.
rule.rationale: Premature access revocation during handover breaks ongoing operations and is harder to reverse than to delay.

section.name: step-3-lessons-learned
section.p: Capture lessons and root causes while the team is still available and memories are fresh.

rule.statement: Conduct the lessons learned session before the team disperses, not after. Schedule it at project kickoff as a fixed deliverable.
rule.rationale: Memories fade within days and teams scatter to new projects within hours of a delivery. A lesson-learned session two weeks post-close captures platitudes, not facts.

rule.statement: Store lessons in a queryable format (structured YAML or database), not in a slide deck. Each lesson: what happened, root cause, recommendation, project context.
rule.rationale: Slide decks are never searched; structured lessons are findable by project type, risk category, or team name.

section.name: steps-4-7-archive-report-transition-recognise
section.p: Finalize and hand over project artefacts, produce the final report, transition operations, and recognize team contributions.

rule.statement: Archive project documents in a structure that matches future search intent: Planning, Execution, Technical, Contracts, Closure. Tag with project type and key outcomes.
rule.rationale: Unstructured archives are effectively deleted — nobody finds them. Structure by intended reuse, not by creation order.

rule.statement: Decommission infrastructure in stages: read-only first, then revoke writes, then deprovision. Default to 30-day soft-delete before hard deletion.
rule.rationale: Staged decommission allows recovery if handover is incomplete. Hard-deletion of cloud resources without a hold period risks irreversible data loss.

rule.statement: Include a team recognition event. Budget it from kickoff as a project line item, not as an afterthought when budgets are exhausted.
rule.rationale: Skipping recognition damages future engagement and signals that completion is the PM's success, not the team's.

section.name: closure-types
section.p: Four closure variants apply to different project end-states:

list.item: Normal — objectives met, full closure process applies.
Early (success) — objectives met ahead of schedule, accelerated closure with same gate sequence.
Early (cancelled) — document completed work percentage, recover reusable assets, record cancellation rationale formally to prevent same idea resurfacing without learning.
Integration — project absorbed into operations, transition-focused closure with extra emphasis on runbooks, monitoring handover, and CMDB updates.

rule.statement: For cancelled projects, document the cancellation rationale in a signed document with the sponsor's name. An informal "we changed direction" leaves the next team without context.
rule.rationale: Cancelled project rationale is the most frequently lost institutional knowledge; it reappears as the same initiative 18 months later with the same failure mode.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when agents conduct closure independently:

antipattern.description: Agent marks closure complete based on issue-tracker "Done" state alone.
antipattern.reason: Done status is self-reported by the team. Acceptance requires a signed artefact from the accepting authority — a different party with independent verification.

antipattern.description: Agent generates lessons learned from ticket history and meeting minutes without human contribution.
antipattern.reason: Agent-generated lessons are pattern-matched platitudes from training data, not project-specific facts. Require quoted contributions attributed to named participants.

antipattern.description: Agent sets final cost to zero because no open invoices appear in the system.
antipattern.reason: Net-60 vendor invoices arrive after project close. Final cost must be flagged as "pending invoices" until the invoice window closes, not auto-zeroed.

antipattern.description: Agent publishes the closure packet without access control.
antipattern.reason: Closure packets contain salaries, vendor rates, and customer data. Restrict access before agents publish or distribute.

section.name: agentic-workflow-and-recommended-agents
section.p: A subagent walks the closure checklist, audits current state of contracts, costs, accesses, and documentation, and produces a closure packet (final report, lessons summary, handover document, archive index). The agent never marks closure complete — it produces the artefacts and a checklist that the PM and sponsor sign off. Treat closure as data: each checklist item is a YAML record with status, owner, evidence link. Tie closure into the SDD lifecycle: feature folder moves in-progress/ → done/ only after closure tasks are green. Pair with Lessons Learned methodology for retrospective facilitation.

list.item: faion-pm-agent — drives checklist, drafts final report.
faion-improver — runs lessons-learned facilitation prompts; mines patterns across closures.
faion-business-analyst — verifies every requirement traces to an accepted deliverable.
faion-sdd-executor-agent — moves SDD feature folder to done/ after closure pass.

section.p: Prompt pattern: inputs include charter.md, scope_baseline.md, schedule_baseline.csv, budget_baseline.csv, current_status.md, deliverables.yaml, contracts.yaml, accesses.yaml. Output: closure_packet/ containing final_report.md, acceptance_log.md, handover.md, archive_index.md, closure_checklist.yaml. Each checklist item includes: id, name, status (pending|done|na), owner, evidence_url, blocker.
Audit closure_checklist.yaml against today's state: report items where status=done but no evidence_url, or pending with no blocker reason. Output: critical-path open items only.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services

list.item: gh, glab, jira-cli — Close issues, archive milestones, export ticket history.
az devops, ado-rest — Close iterations and work items in ADO.
git archive / git bundle — Archive code repository state at closure.
pandoc — Render final report → DOCX/PDF for sponsor sign-off.
aws s3 sync / gsutil rsync / azcopy — Move artefacts to long-term cold storage.
op (1Password CLI) — Revoke project-scoped credentials.
terraform destroy — Decommission temporary infra cleanly.
docusign-cli — Collect electronic acceptance signatures.

section.p: Services and SaaS platforms with agent-friendly APIs for closure:

list.item: Atlassian Confluence (SaaS, REST API) — Archive page tree; mark space read-only.
Notion (SaaS, REST API) — Project DB, change status to "Closed".
DocuSign / Adobe Sign (SaaS, REST API) — Acceptance form signatures.
ServiceNow (SaaS, Table API) — Operations handover ticket; CMDB updates.
Jira / ADO / GitHub Projects (SaaS, REST/GraphQL) — Close epics, archive boards.
1Password (SaaS, op CLI) — Revoke project vault access.
AWS / GCP / Azure (SaaS, provider CLIs) — Decommission infra; budget alerts.
Sharepoint / Google Drive (SaaS, Graph / Drive API) — Archive document library, set retention.
Schedule closure at kickoff: it's a deliverable, not an afterthought.
Acceptance criteria must be observable and signed before closure starts; ambiguous "looks good" causes drift.
Run lessons-learned before the team disperses; afterwards memories fade and the room scatters.
Decommission infrastructure in stages: first read-only, then revoke writes, then deprovision; reverts are easier early.
Archive code with a tagged release named closure-<project> for forensic recovery.
Recover salvageable assets (designs, components, datasets) into a reusable library before deletion.
Celebrate. Skipping recognition damages future engagement; budget a small line item from kickoff.
For cancelled projects, document cancellation rationale formally — it prevents the same idea resurfacing without learning.

template.purpose: Complete closure checklist with all required items grouped by category
Deliverable acceptance form with criteria table and signature block
Operations handover document: system overview, docs, support contacts, known issues

script.purpose: Python auditor: validates closure_checklist.yaml completeness and evidence links
